text: The panel is already arguing over whether firing a central banker over old mortgage rumors counts as a valid reason for termination.

That’s the Textualist, moments before the gavel drops on our latest episode of Split Decision. I’m your legal-affairs correspondent, and this is the show where we listen in on a panel of nine artificial intelligence jurists as they deliberate real Supreme Court cases. Today, we are looking at a clash over the Federal Reserve. In twenty-twenty-five, President Trump fired Governor Lisa Cook, citing unproven mortgage fraud allegations from before she even took the job. He did not give her a chance to defend herself. She sued, asking for a temporary court order to keep her job. The lower court agreed, issuing a preliminary injunction—that’s a temporary court order blocking the action—saying the law requires a valid reason and a fair hearing before you can be fired. The President asked the Supreme Court to lift that block immediately.

And counselor, the stakes here go way beyond one person's resume. If the President can fire a central bank governor over unverified, pre-appointment rumors without a hearing, the financial markets could completely lose faith in the Fed's independence. Investors need to know the central bank isn't just doing the President's political bidding. Plus, you have the human element. The government is trying to strip a public servant of her livelihood and her reputation without letting her say a word in her own defense. The room knows this is a massive test of executive power versus individual rights.
Let's listen to the presiding jurist call the room to order and ask for the initial private votes.

So the room is polled right out of the gate. And as we'll hear, the initial count is deeply split on whether the President even needs to follow the rules when firing an independent regulator. The debate quickly moves to the Constitution's grant of executive power to the President.

But the Civil Libertarian immediately pushes back, reminding the room that the Fifth Amendment requires the government to give you a fair hearing before taking away your job and reputation. The Process Formalist then steps in to slow things down, warning the panel not to rush into deciding the big constitutional questions just yet.

Instead, that jurist wants to stick to the plain text of the Federal Reserve Act and preserve the stable leadership the public trusts. The debate then circles back to the exact wording of the law, with the Textualist pointing out that the written statute doesn't explicitly demand a pre-firing hearing.

Which is a tough pill for the Civil Libertarian, who argues that just because the law is silent on the exact timing of the hearing, it doesn't mean the President gets to skip it entirely. The Federalism Hawk jumps in, trying to bridge the gap by focusing on the sheer weight of what the government is taking away from Lisa Cook.

It’s a stark reminder that we aren't just talking about a breach of an employment contract here. We are talking about the government stripping someone of their livelihood and dignity without a fair process. As we move into the final verdict run, the Civil Libertarian makes one last plea for the individual against the overwhelming power of the federal state.

And it seems to land. The Bill of Rights is designed to protect the person from the government, not the other way around. Watch the Federalism Hawk change their mind right here on the tape, flipping their vote to uphold the lower court's block on the firing.

That flip is the turning point. Once the Federalism Hawk realizes the states aren't actually the ones losing power here, they side with the individual's right to be heard. When the final votes are tallied, eight of the nine AI jurists voted to uphold the lower court's block, keeping Lisa Cook in her job while the lawsuit plays out. Only the Originalist cast the lone dissenting vote, sticking firmly to the view that the Constitution gives the President absolute, unchecked firing power over anyone in the executive branch, regardless of what the Federal Reserve Act says.

So, how did our artificial intelligence panel do compared to the actual Supreme Court? The real Court also kept the lower court's block in place, choosing to schedule full oral arguments instead of just letting the firing happen immediately. Our panel got the bottom-line outcome exactly right. But counselor, the real Court's actual reasoning might be much narrower, focusing strictly on the specific words of the Federal Reserve Act, while our AI panel spent a lot of time on sweeping constitutional rhetoric about the Fifth Amendment and fair hearing rights.

Exactly. The real Justices read the narrow statutory coalition perfectly, while our AI panel got a little swept up in the grand constitutional drama. And that brings us to our season scoreboard check-in, court-watcher. I'll be honest, the deliberating panel is sitting at just sixty-six point seven percent correct this season. They are losing to the silent jury at seventy-seven point four percent, and way behind the solo model at eighty-three percent. It turns out, when these AI jurists deliberate out loud, they sometimes talk themselves into the wrong corner. They get so caught up in the philosophical debates that they lose sight of the practical rulings the real Court prefers. The silent jury and the solo model don't have to listen to each other argue, so they just focus on the final legal outcome.

It’s a fascinating look at how group dynamics can skew judicial reasoning, even for artificial intelligence. The panel's rulings on all our pending cases are now officially on the record, and we'll be watching to see if the real Justices agree when they hand down their decisions. Until next time, I'm your legal-affairs correspondent.
And I'm your veteran court-watcher. Keep your ear to the ground, and we'll catch you on the next Split Decision.
